event: Austronesian voyagers, ancestors of the Chamorro people, settle the Mariana Islands after long-distance ocean migration from Island Southeast Asia, among the earliest sustained open-ocean voyages in human history.
Early Chamorro communities establish pottery-making, fishing, and horticultural villages along the coasts of Saipan, Tinian, and Rota.
The distinctive Latte Period begins, marked by the construction of latte stones, massive stone pillars topped with capstones used to support raised buildings.
Ferdinand Magellan's expedition reaches the Marianas, making the first recorded European contact with the Chamorro people.
Spanish explorer Miguel Lopez de Legazpi formally claims the Mariana Islands for the Spanish Crown.
Jesuit missionary Diego Luis de San Vitores establishes the first Catholic mission and renames the islands Las Marianas in honor of Queen Mariana of Austria.
Father San Vitores is killed by Chamorro chief Mata'pang, sparking decades of conflict between Spanish forces and the Chamorro.
The Spanish-Chamorro Wars end, and surviving Chamorros are forcibly relocated to Guam, leaving the northern islands largely depopulated.
Carolinian islanders begin migrating to the depopulated northern Marianas, particularly Saipan, with Spanish permission, forming a lasting community.
Following the Spanish-American War, Spain sells the Northern Mariana Islands to the German Empire under the German-Spanish Treaty.
Japan seizes the Northern Marianas from Germany at the outbreak of World War I.
The League of Nations grants Japan a South Pacific Mandate over the islands, beginning intensive Japanese administration.
Japan develops Saipan and Tinian into major sugar-producing centers, importing thousands of Japanese, Okinawan, and Korean laborers who come to outnumber the local population.
U.S. forces capture Saipan and Tinian in fierce World War II battles, with mass civilian and military casualties including the cliff-jumping suicides at Marpi Point.
The atomic bombers Enola Gay and Bockscar take off from Tinian's North Field to drop atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki.
The Northern Mariana Islands become part of the U.S.-administered Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands under a United Nations trusteeship.
Saipan becomes the administrative capital of the Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands.
Northern Marianas voters approve a Covenant to establish a political union with the United States as a commonwealth.
The Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands (CNMI) formally comes into being with its own constitution and elected governor, Carlos Camacho.
U.S. citizenship is conferred on the people of the Northern Mariana Islands as the Covenant fully takes effect.
The United Nations Security Council terminates the trusteeship over the Northern Mariana Islands, completing its political transition.
A booming garment industry takes hold, attracting tens of thousands of foreign guest workers and later drawing scrutiny over labor conditions.
The Northern Marianas elects its first nonvoting delegate to the U.S. House of Representatives, Gregorio Kilili Camacho Sablan.
President George W. Bush establishes the Marianas Trench Marine National Monument, protecting deep ocean waters around the northern islands.
Federal immigration control over the CNMI begins transitioning from local to U.S. federal authority under the Consolidated Natural Resources Act.
Typhoon Soudelor devastates Saipan, knocking out power and water and causing widespread destruction across the island.
Super Typhoon Yutu, one of the strongest storms ever to strike U.S. soil, causes catastrophic damage to Tinian and Saipan.
The COVID-19 pandemic devastates the islands' tourism-dependent economy as international travel collapses.
The CNMI continues recovery and faces ongoing challenges from climate change, including sea-level rise, coral bleaching, and intensifying tropical cyclones.
